and 4 of the Understanding on Rules and Procedures Governing the Settlement of Disputes (DSU),

Agreement on Trade-Related Investment Measures (TRIMs Agreement), and Articles 4 and 30 of the

Settlement Body (DSB) established a panel pursuant to the request of China in document
WT/DS623/3, in accordance with Article 6 of the DSU.3

To examine, in the light of the relevant provisions of the covered agreements cited by

2 FACTUAL ASPECTS
2.1 Introduction
2.1. The Inflation Reduction Act6 (IRA) is a statute adopted by the US Congress that was signed
into law on 16 August 2022. Title I, Subtitle D (Energy Security) of the IRA amended the Internal
Revenue Code, the primary source of US federal tax law, by creating and amending a number of tax
credits concerning clean vehicle adoption and renewable energy production and investment. China's
claims in this dispute are directed at "certain subsidies provided under the IRA that are contingent,
in one way or another, upon the use of domestic over imported goods or that otherwise discriminate
against goods of Chinese origin".7
2.2. More specifically, China's panel request challenges two sets of measures. The first is the Clean
Vehicle Tax Credit. China's claims against the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit are directed at certain
eligibility conditions that electric vehicles must meet to qualify for the tax credit. The second set of
measures are the Investment Tax Credits (ITCs) and Production Tax Credits (PTCs) for clean
electricity. As elaborated further below, China's claims against the ITCs and PTCs are directed at the
associated Domestic Content Bonus Credits.
2.2 The United States' termination of the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit and China's withdrawal
of its claims against that measure
2.3. As enacted, the IRA provided that the tax credit would be available for new clean vehicles
placed into service until 31 December 2032. However, the United States' One Big Beautiful Bill Act
(OBBBA), as signed into law on 4 July 2025, terminates the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit in respect of
electric vehicles acquired after 30 September 2025.8
2.4. In the context of its comments on the United States' responses to the panel's questions
following the second meeting, filed on 3 October 2025, China indicated that it was withdrawing its
claims against the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit. Specifically, China explained that:
As the parties discussed at the second substantive meeting, and as the Panel is aware,
the Clean Vehicle Credit was terminated effective 30 September 2025 as a result of the
One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). As this termination has now taken effect, China
considers that it is no longer necessary for the Panel to make findings or
recommendations in respect of this measure. China therefore withdraws its claims under
Articles I:1 and III:4 of the GATT 1994, Articles 2.1 and 2.2 of the TRIMs Agreement,
and Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2 of the SCM Agreement in respect of the Clean Vehicle Credit,
and requests that the Panel decline to make findings or recommendations concerning
these claims.9
2.5. In its subsequent comments, filed on 10 October 2025, the United States indicated that China's
withdrawal of its claims against the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit means that China has effectively
withdrawn the matter with respect to the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit from its panel request to the
DSB.10 According to the United States, as China has withdrawn the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit from
the matter referred to the DSB, the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit is now excluded from the Panel's terms
of reference, and it is no longer permissible for the Panel to make findings or recommendations on
these claims.11
2.6. In the light of China's withdrawal of its claims and request that the Panel decline to make
findings or recommendations concerning its claims against the Clean Vehicle Tax Credit, this

2.3 The ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits
2.8. China challenges aspects of the Investment Tax Credits (ITCs) and Production Tax Credits
(PTCs) for clean electricity. We refer to these aspects as the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus
Credits.14 We note that certain US government agencies have issued implementing regulations
and/or other guidance documents related to the challenged aspects of the ITCs and PTCs that are
contained in the US Code. In particular, on 12 May 2023, the IRS issued the "Domestic Content
Bonus Credit Guidance under Sections 45, 45Y, 48, and 48E", which sets forth proposed guidance
equally applicable to the four ITCs and PTCs discussed below.15
2.9. The ITCs incentivize the initial development of new renewable energy projects (e.g. solar
farms) by providing tax credits that reduce upfront costs of investment in such facilities. The IRA
extended the application of the pre-existing ITC (codified at 26 U.S.C. Section 48) and introduced a
new Investment Tax Credit for Clean Electricity (codified at 26 U.S.C. Section 48E). The pre-existing
ITC codified at 26 U.S.C. Section 48 applies to a given "energy property" defined to cover a variety
of renewable energy projects that began construction before 1 January 2025 (with certain
exceptions).16 The new ITC for clean electricity applies to "qualified facilities" defined as those that
produce electricity, have anticipated greenhouse gas emissions rates of zero, and are placed into
service after 31 December 2024; it also applies to "energy storage technology".17
2.10. The PTCs incentivize ongoing renewable electricity production by providing tax credits for
qualified facilities for generally the first ten years of operation. The IRA extended the application of
the pre-existing PTC, codified at 26 U.S.C. Section 45 and in general applicable to electricity from
facilities for which construction begins before 2025, and introduced a new PTC, codified at 26 U.S.C.
Section 45Y and applicable to electricity generation facilities that are placed in service after
31 December 2024. The pre-existing PTC applied to electricity produced from "qualified energy
resources" and "at a qualified facility" and defined "Qualified facilities" to cover a variety of renewable
energy products.18 The new PTC for clean electricity defines a "qualified facility" as, generally, one
that is placed into service after 31 December 2024 and produces electricity that has zero greenhouse
gas emissions.19
2.11. The amount of the tax credits granted under the ITCs/PTCs vary depending on the
circumstances, and certain additional "bonus" tax credit portions are also available. Generally
speaking, there is a "base" rate for qualifying projects. For the ITCs, this base rate is usually 6% of

five (i.e., to 30 percent for ITCs and to 1.5 cents/kWh for PTCs), where a project satisfies certain
"prevailing wage and apprenticeship" requirements, subject to limited exceptions.22
2.12. In this dispute, China challenges the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits. We refer to
the Domestic Content Bonus Credits in the plural because the nature and method of the credit differ
as between the ITCs and PTCs. Generally, they operate as follows: (a) in the context of the ITCs,
the domestic content bonus credit amounts to up to additional ten percentage points of the total
qualified capital costs of constructing the eligible clean energy facility; (b) in the context of the PTCs,
the domestic content bonus credit amounts to the otherwise applicable PTC being increased by
10%.23
2.13. Thus, in both instances, the Domestic Content Bonus Credit basically amounts to a 10% bonus
increase of the otherwise available tax credit amount.24 The Domestic Content Bonus Credits of the
ITCs and PTCs are contingent on meeting two domestic content requirements. Both must be fulfilled,
and the same domestic content requirements apply to all the ITCs and PTCs:
a. The first domestic content requirement is the Steel and Iron Requirement. A project must
use 100% domestic steel and iron to the extent that construction materials are made
primarily of steel or iron and are structural in nature. This might include, in the case of a
solar farm, steel module racking and steel or iron rebar in the foundation. In the case of
a land-based or offshore wind turbine facility, this might include the tower and the steel
or iron rebar in the foundation.25
b. The second domestic content requirement is the Manufactured Products Requirement. A
specified percentage of manufactured goods that become part of an electricity generation
facility (e.g. solar panels, wind turbine blades) must be mined, produced or manufactured
in the United States. The percentage threshold is 40% for projects that begin construction
before 2025 and increases by five percentage points per year up to 55% for projects
starting construction in 2027 and beyond. For offshore wind facilities, the schedule starts
at 20% and steps up to 55% for construction projects beginning after 2027.26

2.15. The ITCs and PTCs for renewable energy are federal income tax credits that are claimed as
"general business credits" under 26 U.S.C. Section 38.28 Section 38 states that these credits "shall
be allowed as a credit against the tax imposed by this chapter for the taxable year".29 The credits
thus reduce the total federal income tax liability of a taxpayer. If the credit exceeds the tax liability,
unused credits can typically be carried back one year or forward up to 20 years to offset taxes in
other years.30 In addition to the default option of treating the ITCs/PTCs as tax credits, there are
two other options to obtain the benefits contained in the ITCs and PTCs: (a) the "direct pay" option
allows certain entities (esp. tax-exempt entities) to treat these tax credits as refundable payments
of tax, in which case they may receive a direct payment from the IRS for any amount paid in excess
of their tax liability31; and (b) the "transfer" option allows eligible taxpayers to transfer all or a
portion of the ITC and PTC to an unrelated party for cash payment.32 Thus, while we refer to the
measures as the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits, we recognize that they can be structured
in ways that could be characterized other than as a tax credit.

7 FINDINGS
7.1 Introduction and order of analysis
7.1. China's claims against the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are directed at certain
eligibility conditions which, if met, increase the amounts of the investment and production tax credits
otherwise available to clean electricity producers. As set out in section 2 of this Report, the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits are conditional upon the fulfilment of: (1) the Steel and Iron
Requirement, and (2) the Manufactured Products Requirement. We do not repeat our factual
description here.
7.2. China claims that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with the
national treatment obligations in Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 and Articles 2.1 and 2.2 of the TRIMs
Agreement. China further claims that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are subsidies
contingent on the use of domestic over imported goods in violation of Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2 of the
SCM Agreement.
7.3. The United States does not dispute that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are
inconsistent with the provisions of the GATT 1994, the TRIMs Agreement, and the SCM Agreement
referred to by China. However, the United States requests that the Panel reject all of China's claims
against the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits, on the grounds that the ITC/PTC Domestic
Content Bonus Credits are justified under Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994 as measures necessary to
protect public morals.

7.4. The order of analysis is informed by the manner in which the complainant has presented its
claims, and equally, by the manner in which the respondent has presented its defences.44 In its first
written submission, China presents its claims under the GATT 1994 first, followed by its claims under
the TRIMs Agreement, followed then by its claims under the SCM Agreement. The United States
does not contest that the measures are inconsistent with the provisions referred to by China; it
defends the measures by invoking the public morals exception in Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994 in
respect of all the claims advanced.
7.5. In the circumstances of these proceedings, we see no reason to deviate from the order of
analysis followed by China in its first written submission. We have considered the United States'
suggestion that we begin our analysis with the claims under the SCM Agreement to efficiently resolve
and fully address the claims at issue.45 We agree with the United States that where a panel finds
that a measure is a subsidy contingent on the use of domestic over imported products under Articles
3.1(b) and 3.2 of the SCM Agreement, such a finding would in principle serve to establish that the
same measure is necessarily inconsistent with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 and Article 2.1 of the
TRIMs Agreement, and potentially obviate the need to make further findings under the GATT 1994
and the TRIMs Agreement.46 However, in the circumstances of this dispute we do not consider that
exercising economy over the claims under the GATT 1994 and the TRIMs Agreement – which are
straightforward and uncontested – would make any appreciable contribution to the efficient
resolution of the dispute. Furthermore, while we appreciate the logic of the United States'
suggestion, it appears that in most other disputes involving parallel claims under the overlapping
obligations47 in the GATT 1994, the TRIMs Agreement, and the SCM Agreement, panels have ordered
their analysis to address claims under the GATT 1994 and the TRIMs Agreement before addressing
prohibited subsidy claims under the SCM Agreement.48 We consider that a panel's order of analysis
may be informed by case-specific considerations. Having said that, the approach followed by past
panels is relevant, insofar as it concerns the same issue we are confronted with here; and the
approach followed by most past panels persuades us that the order of analysis followed by China
raises no legal or logical problem, and may in fact facilitate the efficient resolution of the several
claims raised in this dispute. As such, we see no reason not to follow this same approach in the
present dispute.
7.6. Accordingly, we order our analysis to begin with China's claims under the GATT 1994, followed
by China's claims under the TRIMs Agreement, and then address China's claims under the SCM
Agreement. We will then address the United States' defence under the public morals exception in
Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994, which it invokes as a response to all of China's claims.
7.7. Before doing so, we note that, as discussed in section 2.3 above, the ITCs and PTCs are worth
certain monetary amounts, which the Domestic Content Bonus Credits can then increase. Because
the Domestic Content Bonus Credits operate to increase the amount of the ITCs and PTCs, they are
therefore intertwined, both practically and legislatively, with the ITCs and PTCs more generally.
Accordingly, at times in our analyses below we will necessarily speak of the ITCs and PTCs more
generally when assessing the design, structure, and content of these measures, and how they
function. We consider it useful to recall that China does not challenge the ITCs and PTCs generally,
and any findings of inconsistency with the covered agreements in this Report are only made with
respect to the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits.

7.2 Claims under the GATT 1994
7.2.1 Introduction
7.8. China claims that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with
Article III:4 because by making eligibility for bonus subsidy amounts conditional on the use of US-
origin goods, the United States does not accord to products of Chinese origin treatment no less
favourable than the treatment accorded to like products of national origin in respect of laws,
regulations, and requirements affecting the internal sale, offering for sale, purchase, transportation,
distribution or use of the affected products.49
7.9. The United States does not contest China's claim.
7.10. Although the United States does not contest China's claims, we cannot summarily conclude
that those claims are well-founded; we must make an objective assessment of whether China has
made a prima facie case.50 This section proceeds in two parts. First, it recalls the applicable legal
standard under Article III:4. Second, it applies that legal standard to the ITC/PTC Domestic Content
Bonus Tax Credits.
7.2.2 Applicable legal standard
7.11. Article III of the GATT 1994 is entitled "National Treatment on Internal Taxation and
Regulation". Article III:4 provides, in relevant part, that:
The products of the territory of any Member imported into the territory of any other
Member shall be accorded treatment no less favourable than that accorded to like
products of national origin in respect of all laws, regulations and requirements affecting
their internal sale, offering for sale, purchase, transportation, distribution or use.
7.12. China must demonstrate three elements to establish a violation of Article III:4: first, that the
imported and domestic products at issue are "like" products; second, that the eligibility conditions
are "laws, regulations, or requirements" affecting the internal sale, offering for sale, purchase,
transportation, distribution or use of the relevant products; and third, that the eligibility conditions
accord to imported products treatment that is "less favourable" than the treatment accorded to like
domestic products.
7.13. Regarding the first element, the likeness evaluation generally takes account of the products'
end-uses; consumers' tastes and habits; the physical properties of the products, including their
nature and quality; and the tariff classification of the products. However, where a measure
distinguishes between the two classes of products exclusively on the basis of origin, the likeness of
the two classes of products is presumed, without the need to take the foregoing criteria into
account.51
7.14. On the second element, the reference to "laws, regulations, or requirements" in Article III:4
includes requirements that an enterprise voluntarily undertakes in order to obtain an advantage
from the government, including tax advantages or other forms of subsidy.52 The requirement that
"laws, regulations, or requirements … affect the internal sale, offering for sale, purchase,
transportation, distribution or use of the relevant products" has been interpreted broadly to
encompass measures that affect the equality of competitive opportunities for imported vis-à-vis like

51 Panel Reports, China – Publications and Audiovisual Products, para. 7.1446 ("when origin is the sole

domestic products.53 These include tax advantages, indigenization requirements, and other similar
measures that create an incentive for the use of domestic over imported products.54
7.15. With respect to the third element, the treatment accorded to like imported products under a
measure at issue is less favourable if that treatment "modifies the conditions of competition in the
relevant market to the detriment of imported products".55 The fact that a measure modifies the
conditions of competition to the detriment of imported products is, in itself, sufficient for a finding
that the measure confers less favourable treatment.56 A finding of less favourable treatment "need
not be based on the actual effects of the contested measure in the marketplace".57 Nor does it
"require demonstration of trade effects, nor proof that the sourcing decisions of private firms have
actually been impacted" by the measure or measures at issue.58 This is because "Article III protects
expectations not of any particular trade volume but rather of equal competitive relationships
between imported and domestic products."59
7.2.3 Application to the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits
7.16. We recall that the ITCs and PTCs are available to use as tax credits.60 The four ITCs and PTCs
have materially identical Domestic Content Bonus Credits, which are based on the Steel and Iron
Requirement and the Manufactured Products Requirement. The former requires that domestic steel
and iron be used in the construction of relevant facilities, and the latter requires that a certain
amount of other domestic products be used in the construction of such facilities. Only if both such
requirements are satisfied do the Domestic Content Bonus Credits become available, which
essentially increase the amount of the ITC or PTC by either 10 percentage points or 10%,
respectively. These are therefore conditions requiring the use of domestic over imported goods in
the text of the measures themselves.
7.17. In the case of the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits, we consider that the likeness of
the relevant domestic and imported products may be presumed because the eligibility conditions
make distinctions exclusively on the basis of origin. In respect of steel and iron products falling
within the scope of the Steel and Iron Requirement, the relevant eligibility conditions for obtaining
a Domestic Content Bonus Credit relate exclusively to whether the products in question are of US
origin. Similarly, for other manufactured products falling within the scope of the Manufactured
Products Requirement, the relevant eligibility conditions for obtaining the Domestic Content Bonus
Credits also relate exclusively to whether the products in question are of US origin.
7.18. We consider that the challenged eligibility conditions constitute requirements in the sense of
Article III:4 because they are requirements that an enterprise must fulfil to obtain an advantage
from the government – namely, the advantage of obtaining an increase in the amount of the ITC or
PTC by either 10 percentage points or 10%, respectively. Furthermore, these requirements "affect
the internal sale, offering for sale, purchase, … distribution or use" of the relevant products – in this
case, iron or steel, or other manufactured products, used in the subject project – because these
requirements create an incentive to sell, offer for sale, purchase, or use domestic over like imported
products of these types.

7.19. Finally, we consider that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits accord less favourable
treatment to like imported products relative to the treatment accorded to qualifying domestic
products. We note that less favourable treatment has consistently been found in the case of
measures that provide tax incentives, subsidies, administrative advantages, or other similar benefits
for the use of domestic products over like imported products.61 Importantly, "less favourable
treatment" exists under these circumstances even where there are other means of obtaining the
favourable treatment that do not require the use of domestic over imported goods.62
7.20. We therefore find that China has made a prima facie case that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content
Bonus Credits are inconsistent with Article III:4.
7.2.4 Conclusion
7.21. For the reasons stated above, we conclude that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits
are inconsistent with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 because by making eligibility for bonus subsidy
amounts conditional on the use of US-origin goods, the United States does not accord to products
of Chinese origin treatment no less favourable than the treatment accorded to like products of
national origin in respect of laws, regulations, and requirements affecting the internal sale, offering
for sale, purchase, transportation, distribution or use of the affected products.

7.4 Claims under the SCM Agreement
7.4.1 Introduction
7.40. We have upheld China's claims that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are
inconsistent with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 and Article 2.1 of the TRIMs Agreement. We now
examine China's claims under Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2 the SCM Agreement.
7.41. China claims that ITCs and PTCs, including as increased by the Domestic Content Bonus
Credits, are subsidies within the meaning of Article 1 of the SCM Agreement, and are contingent on
the use of domestic over imported goods within the meaning of Articles 3.1(b) of the SCM Agreement
insofar as the Domestic Content Bonus Credits increase their value.
7.42. The United States contests neither issue.
7.43. Although the United States does not contest China's claims, we must make an objective
assessment that China has made a prima facie case.80 This section proceeds in two parts. First, it
recalls the applicable legal standards under Articles 1 and 3 of the SCM Agreement. Second, it
examines whether the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are subsidies contingent on the use
of domestic over imported goods.
7.4.2 Applicable legal standards
7.4.2.1 Subsidy: Article 1 of the SCM Agreement
7.44. Article 1.1 of the SCM Agreement provide, in relevant part:
For the purpose of this Agreement, a subsidy shall be deemed to exist if:
(a)(1) there is a financial contribution by a government or any public body
within the territory of a Member (referred to in this Agreement as
"government"), i.e. where:
(i) a government practice involves a direct transfer of funds (e.g.
grants, loans, and equity infusion), potential direct transfers of funds
or liabilities (e.g. loan guarantees);
(ii) government revenue that is otherwise due is foregone or not
collected (e.g. fiscal incentives such as tax credits);

7.45. Regarding Article 1.1(a)(1)(i), the phrase "a government practice involv[ing] a direct transfer
of funds" has been interpreted as capturing "conduct on the part of the government by which money,
financial resources, and/or financial claims are made available to a recipient". The meaning of
"funds" therefore includes not only money, but also financial resources and other financial claims
more generally.81 Additionally, the phrase may encompass situations where a government or public
body transfers funds to a recipient through an intermediary, depending on the nature and role of
the intermediary and whether there is a resulting sufficient degree of "directness".82
7.46. To determine if government revenue is "otherwise due" under Article 1.1(a)(1)(ii), a panel is
required to:
a. identify the tax treatment that applies to the alleged subsidy recipients;
b. identify a benchmark for comparison (which involves identifying tax treatment of
comparable income of comparably situated taxpayers); and
c. compare the challenged treatment with the benchmark tax treatment.83
7.47. Because transactions can be complex, different aspects of a transaction may be properly
characterized as different types of financial contributions, and a transaction may be properly
characterized as more than one type of financial contribution.84
7.48. A financial contribution confers a benefit when it makes the recipient better off than it would
otherwise have been, absent that contribution.85 While a financial contribution and a benefit are
separate legal elements in Article 1.1, as multiple panels have observed, where a financial
contribution exists in the form of revenue forgone otherwise due, a finding of a benefit – and hence
a subsidy – readily follows, i.e. the amount of tax that was forgone.86 With respect to financial
contributions in the form of direct transfer of funds, the benefit analysis will depend on the specific
form that such a financial contribution takes. However, where the financial contribution takes the
form of, for example, a grant or similar instrument lacking reciprocal obligations, a finding of a
benefit will also readily follow because such payments are generally unavailable in the market.87
7.49. Article 2.3 of the SCM Agreement provides that subsidies falling under Article 3 are deemed
to be specific. Since China claims that the relevant subsidies are prohibited subsidies under Article 3,
we need not separately address whether the alleged subsidies are specific under Article 2.

7.54. The record reflects that the ITCs and PTCs can be used in the three different ways that China
describes (i.e. the Investor/Producer Credit, the Elective Payment, and the Assignment Credit). This
section analyses each in turn. Because of the similarity of the Domestic Content Bonus Credits under
each ITC and PTC, and the similarity with which US law treats the ITCs and PTCs95, the following
analyses apply equally to each of the four ITCs and PTCs.
7.4.3.1.2 Investor/Producer Credit
7.55. As a default, the ITCs and PTCs are available to use as tax credits.96 China argues that, used
in this way, the ITCs and PTCs are financial contributions in the form of revenue forgone otherwise
due under Article 1.1(a)(1)(ii) of the SCM Agreement. The United States does not contest this
characterization. This section therefore evaluates this scenario under the three-step process
described in paragraph 7.46 above.
7.56. First, we identify the challenged tax treatment. In this context, the ITCs and PTCs are federal
income tax credits that are claimed as "general business credits" under 26 U.S.C. Section 38.97 That
section states that these credits "shall be allowed as a credit against the tax imposed by this chapter
for the taxable year".98 The phrase "this chapter" refers to Chapter 1 of the Internal Revenue Code,
entitled "normal taxes and surtaxes", which includes tax on individuals and corporations.99 If the
credit exceeds the tax liability, it is generally carried back one year or forward up to 20 years to
offset taxes in other years.100 The ITCs and PTCs thus reduce the total federal tax liability of a
taxpayer.101
7.57. Second, China argues that the benchmark for comparison is the set of normal tax rules that
apply in the absence of the credit. More specifically, the benchmark according to China is Chapter 1
of the Internal Revenue Code, entitled "normal taxes and surtaxes", which includes tax on individuals
and corporations.102 We accept this argument. Comparably situated taxpayers, in our view, are
essentially those who invest in electricity production or produce electricity but do not qualify for the
ITCs and PTCs or the Domestic Content Bonus Credits. The ITCs and PTCs lower the tax liability
under otherwise applicable tax rules for a specified subset of projects.103 This basic structure of the
US tax code is evidenced by the wording of the credits, reducing tax liability under normal tax rules
contained in the applicable code. We further discern no evidence on the record indicating – and the
United States has not argued – that providing similar tax credits or tax reductions for electricity
generation and investment, generally, or further increasing such credits or reductions for using US
domestic steel, iron, and manufactured products in doing so, specifically, are organizing principles
of the US tax code.
7.58. Finally, we note that, in instances where the taxpayer uses the ITCs and PTCs, generally, and
the Domestic Content Bonus Credits, specifically, to lower the taxpayer's federal tax liability, the
taxpayer pays less tax than if the taxpayer were unable to claim the credits. There is further no
evidence that this difference is reflective of any general organizing principle of the US tax system
other than to provide targeted exceptions to otherwise "normal" tax rules. This comparison therefore
reveals that the ITCs and PTCs, including when increased by the Domestic Content Bonus Credits,
represent financial contributions in the form of revenue forgone otherwise due within the meaning
of Article 1.1(a)(1)(ii).

7.60. Certain applicable entities can elect to treat the ITCs and PTCs as deemed payments against
federal income tax liability, rather than as a tax credit.105 The payments first offset any federal tax
due and then any remaining amount of the deemed payment is refunded to the applicable entity.106
The Elective Payment option can be used by tax-exempt entities as well as entities that may have
federal income tax due.107 In light of that background, according to China, the Elective Payment
option can result in one of two different financial contributions, depending on the applicable entity's
tax liability.
7.61. First, China asserts that if the applicable entity has no tax liability, or less tax liability than
the value of the deemed payment, then the refunded portion of the deemed payment is a financial
contribution in the form of a direct transfer of funds under Article 1.1(a)(1)(i) from the government
to the applicable entity. We agree. When the government refunds all or a portion of the deemed
payment (which is just the value of the tax credit, not an actual payment of money) to the applicable
entity in the form of an actual payment of money, this is a direct transfer of funds from the
government to the applicable entity. A benefit is conferred in the amount of the payment from the
government, which essentially resembles a grant lacking reciprocal obligations, making the recipient
better off in the amount of the payment.108
7.62. Second, China asserts that when the government applies any portion of the deemed payment
to the applicable entity's federal income tax liability, and thus retains that portion rather than
refunding it, that portion is revenue forgone otherwise due under Article 1.1(a)(1)(ii). We agree. In
this situation, the retention of the deemed payment applied against any tax due is functionally
indistinguishable from a tax credit. However, instead of lowering initial tax liability like a credit, it
simply counts as payment against an existing tax liability. In our minds, therefore, in this situation,
the analysis in paragraphs 7.55-7.59 above, applies mutatis mutandis. The only material difference
is that the challenged tax treatment here is not a tax credit, but simply a deemed payment of tax
that exists under the normal tax rules. The benchmark is that set of normal tax rules as described
in paragraph 7.57 above, with comparably situated taxpayers also being the same as described in
that paragraph. Observations in that paragraph regarding the structure of the US tax code and its
organizing principles apply equally here. The comparison between the challenged tax treatment and
the benchmark also yields the same result for the same reasons, i.e. the reduction in tax is not
reflective of any organizing principle of the US tax code, thus resulting in a finding of revenue forgone
otherwise due in the amount of the retained portion of the deemed payment.109 A benefit is conferred
in the amount of the lowered tax liability by virtue of the Elective Payment, leaving the taxpayer
better off than in the payment's absence.110

to (or not to) provide an unspecified amount of cash for the assigned credit. Nonetheless, we
consider that the indicia of "directness" of the transfer is still sufficient under these specific
circumstances. This is so because the statute foresees and mandates this flow of value from the
government to the assignor and mandates the forms that the value takes (a tax credit essentially
converted to a cash payment). The assignor ultimately receives cash, i.e. "funds". Overall, therefore,
the law coherently regulates a series of transactions that inevitably results in the transfer of funds
to the assignor. We consider this so even though the provision of cash to the assignor is not strictly
mandated, because we consider it highly unlikely that the assignor will ignore strong and clear
commercial incentives to not give away the valuable credit for free.116

otherwise have been, absent that contribution.117 We consider that when the ITC/PTC Domestic
Content Bonus Credits are used as an Assignment Credit, a benefit is conferred by the cash payment,
leaving the assignor better off than in the payment's absence.

contest this issue.

7.5.1 Introduction
7.71. We have upheld China's claims that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are
inconsistent with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994, Article 2 of the TRIMs Agreement, and
Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2 of the SCM Agreement. The United States argues that the measures are

7.72. According to the United States, the measures at issue protect the United States' public morals

sector[]".119 The United States submits that this dominance has been achieved through "non-market
and trade distorting behaviour, including unfair competition, the use of forced labour, theft, and
coercion", which violate prevailing US standards of right and wrong as reflected in US state and
federal laws.120 In the United States' view, the measures at issue protect public morals by reducing
reliance on China and enhancing fair competition and market-oriented outcomes in the US market121,
by incentivizing production in the United States and in other countries that align with the
United States' public morals122, and by encouraging US consumers to make choices that do not
reward Chinese conduct that is contrary to the United States' public morals.123 The United States
maintains that the measures are "necessary" to protect public morals because previous efforts by
both the United States and other countries, including previous versions of the measures at issue,

the use of non-market policies and practices, and have not enabled the United States or other
countries to maintain or develop capacity in those sectors.124 Finally, the United States argues that
the measures at issue are consistent with the chapeau of Article XX because the same conditions do
not prevail in the United States and China, and because the measures do not constitute a disguised
restriction on international trade.125

7.5.2 Applicable legal standard under Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994
7.5.2.1 General considerations
7.76. Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994 provides:
Subject to the requirement that such measures are not applied in a manner which would
constitute a means of arbitrary or unjustifiable discrimination between countries where
the same conditions prevail, or a disguised restriction on international trade, nothing in
this Agreement shall be construed to prevent the adoption or enforcement by any
[Member] of measures:
(a) necessary to protect public morals;
7.77. The burden of proving that a measure is justified under Article XX rests on the party invoking
that provision.133 This means, in these proceedings, that the United States bears the burden of
demonstrating that the challenged measures satisfy the requirements of both subparagraph (a) and
the chapeau of Article XX. In assessing the parties' arguments and evidence in this respect, we are
mindful that:
[T]he nature and scope of arguments and evidence required to establish a prima facie
case will necessarily vary according to the facts of the case, and from measure to
measure, provision to provision, and case-to-case. Moreover, these rules and principles
of WTO jurisprudence must not be applied in an unduly formalistic or mechanistic
fashion, nor inhibit the substantive analysis that must be undertaken by a panel.134
7.78. Provisional justification under one of the subparagraphs has been understood to require that
a challenged measure address the particular interest specified in that paragraph, and that there be
a sufficient nexus between the measure and the interest protected. In the context of Article XX(a),
this has been understood to mean that a Member seeking to demonstrate that a challenged measure

7.79. The contours of each of these steps or elements of the applicable legal standard under

7.5.2.2 Threshold requirement to show that a measure "protect[s] public morals"
7.80. We note that although the United States is right to point out that the words "designed to" do
not appear in the text of Article XX(a) 137, the word "to" in adverbial relation with the infinitive verb
"protect" is a formulation that "indicates a purpose or intention" and "[t]hus establishes a required
link between the measure and the protected interest".138 The ordinary meaning of the terms of
Article XX(a) therefore supports the approach taken in prior dispute settlement reports considering
an Article XX(a) defence, which has been to conduct an initial, threshold examination of whether the
measures at issue were "designed to" or "taken to" protect public morals, before moving on to
consider whether the measures were "necessary" for that purpose.139 Furthermore, an examination
of whether a measure is designed to pursue the claimed objective seems to us to be a necessary
prerequisite to any examination of the contribution it makes to that asserted objective and/or to an
assessment of whether any less trade-restrictive alternative measures exist that would contribute
to that objective (i.e. whether it is "necessary").140
7.81. Indeed, dispensing with any assessment of whether a measure was in fact designed or taken
"to protect public morals" would be tantamount to a panel treating itself as bound by the
respondent's assertion as to the challenged measure's objective, even when the issue is disputed
and in the face of conflicting evidence and argumentation from the complaining party. However, it
is well established, in the context of Article XX and more generally, that while a panel must take into
account how the respondent has articulated the measure's objectives, it is not bound to accept that
characterization: rather, a panel must make an objective and independent assessment141 of the

connection with both steps of the analysis does not render the "designed to" analysis inutile or

7.5.2.3 Scope and nature of the assessment required
7.86. Having concluded that we must assess whether the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits
are measures "to protect public morals", as an initial, threshold step before proceeding with an
assessment of whether they are "necessary" to protect public morals, we next discuss the scope and
nature of the required assessment.
7.87. The nature of the specific issues to be considered by a panel in the context of this first step
under Article XX will necessarily depend on the circumstances of each case. There have been some
disputes involving Article XX(b) in which the first step has entailed substantial findings on disputed
scientific or other factual issues relating to the existence of the alleged "conduct" that the measure
aims to reduce.153 There have been some disputes in which the first step entailed substantial findings
on direct evidence relating to the stated or declared aims and objectives of the challenged measures,
as evidenced from such sources as the text of laws and regulations, the measure's legislative history,
stated intentions of lawmakers, and surrounding circumstances.154 In most disputes, the analysis
has focused more on the extent to which there was any objectively discernible relationship between
the stated objective and the design, structure, and expected operation of the measure. In some
cases, the lack of any such discernible relationship has been regarded as dispositive when the design,
structure, and expected operation revealed that the measure was incapable of contributing to the
declared objectives.155
7.88. We consider that there may be circumstances in which a panel's assessment could properly
be limited to examining whether a measure is "incapable of" protecting public morals. Notably, in
cases where the argumentation and evidence in relation to this first step substantially overlaps with
the argumentation and evidence that is pertinent to examining the "contribution" step under the
"necessity" test, it might be appropriate to limit the threshold assessment in this manner to avoid
prematurely truncating the "necessity" analysis. However, we do not consider that a panel's
consideration of the extent to which a measure relates to the alleged objective, let alone the entire
first step of assessing whether a measure is one "to protect public morals", should invariably be
circumscribed to an assessment of whether a measure is "incapable of" protecting public morals.

7.90. However, the fact that a challenged measure may be "not incapable" of making a contribution
to a stated objective – even if only accidentally, coincidentally, or hypothetically – does not mean
or imply that the measure was taken or designed for that objective. To accept the opposite would,
in our view, be to conflate a measure's possible outcome or effect with its purpose.
7.91. Indeed, if such a truncated analysis were to exhaust the scope of the initial, threshold step of
the assessment whether a measure was "designed to" address the particular interest specified in

be inconsistent with past practice, which has consistently adopted a two-step analytical framework.

7.5.3.3 Direct evidence of the objectives of the measures at issue
7.140. When assessing whether a challenged measure is designed or taken to protect public morals,
it is logical to ascertain whether the relevant measure makes explicit or direct reference to that
objective. Such a reference may not be dispositive, and we agree with past WTO adjudicators that
a measure may fall within the scope of the public morals exception even if the legal instruments
implementing the measure do not expressly mention a public morals objective.261 Nevertheless, the
presence or absence of such express mention is pertinent.262 Moreover, insofar as there is direct
evidence that the objectives of a measure are different from the objective asserted by the parties,
such evidence may be regarded as highly relevant.
7.141. There have been some disputes in which the first step of the analysis under a subparagraph
of Article XX entailed findings, sometimes substantial, on direct evidence relating to the stated or
declared aims and objectives of the challenged measures, as evidenced from such sources as the

the statements is the effect of Chinese policies and practices on domestic growth and manufacturing
and the stability of the supply of important products. Thus, where concern is expressed in the
statements about Chinese policies and practices, it is not because these are in themselves morally
objectionable, but because of their economic effects on domestic manufacturing and stability of
supply. Put another way, the statements portray the IRA as a response to an economic rather than
a moral hazard.

policies as "non-market", her speech does not evince any concern about the moral impact of these
practices on the society of the United States. Secretary Raimondo thus notes that China's practices

domestic goals, they are not synonymous with or assimilable to the objective of protecting public

measures are designed or taken to protect public morals. If anything, the statements provided are
direct evidence that the measures were taken for other objectives.
7.159. For these reasons, we consider that the direct evidence pertaining to the objectives of the
measures at issue submitted by the United States contradicts its assertion that the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits are measures taken to protect public morals.

7.5.3.4 Design, structure, and expected operation
7.160. In most disputes, the analysis of the initial, threshold step of whether a measure is one "to
protect public morals" has focused mostly on the extent to which there was any objectively
discernible relationship between the stated objective and the challenged measure, taking into
account relevant aspects of the design, structure, and expected operation of the measure. We agree
with prior WTO adjudicators that measures that do not expressly refer to public morals may
nevertheless be found to have a relationship with public morals following an assessment of their
design, content, structure and expected operation, if such assessment demonstrates the existence
of an objectively discernible relationship between the measure and the stated objective.278 It is to
these aspects of the challenged measures that we now turn.
7.161. For clarity, we will consider in turn each of the requirements that must be satisfied in order
to obtain the bonus credits. We recall that each of these domestic content requirements has been
explained in the descriptive part of this Report.
7.5.3.4.1 The Steel and Iron Requirement
7.162. The United States argues that the Steel and Iron Requirement is designed to protect public
morals because the effects of China's non-market policies and practices have been particularly
profound in the steel sector, and this problem persists despite the imposition of numerous trade
remedy measures and longstanding global dialogues. According to the United States, by requiring
the use of 100% US-produced steel and iron for construction materials, the Steel and Iron
Requirement protects the United States' public morals by counteracting the effects of China's non-
market policies and practices.279 The United States has further explained that the Steel and Iron
Requirement, together with the Manufactured Products Requirement, is designed to protect public
morals because they are "structured so as to avoid purchasers rewarding practices that violate the
United States' public morals".280 In this connection, the United States argues that the measures are
necessary as they "signal to businesses in the U.S. marketplace that China's policies will not be
tolerated in the U.S. marketplace, thereby reducing the incentive for actors in the U.S. marketplace
to adopt or encourage behavior similar to that of China, and reducing the reward that Chinese
entities may gain from engaging in that behavior".281
7.163. Additionally, the United States has suggested that the measures are designed to encourage
investments in the United States and like-minded partners to stand up alternative supply and supply
chains to China.282 However, in response to a question from the Panel, the United States clarified its
position on this issue, arguing that "[t]he investment and production bonus credits relate to the U.S.
content of steel, iron, and manufactured products, and are not designed to incentivize production in
other countries that are aligned with US public morals."283
7.164. We do not agree with the United States' assertion that the Steel and Iron Requirement can
be said to "counteract" Chinese policies and practices, or that it is structured to send a signal to the
US market about the moral status of Chinese goods or avoid purchasers rewarding policies and
practices that violate the United States' public morals. Our reasons are as follows.
7.165. In the first place, we recall that the Steel and Iron Requirement does not operate in isolation.
It is one of two requirements that must be fulfilled to obtain the domestic content bonus credits (the
other being the Manufactured Products Requirement) under the ITCs and PTCs. The domestic content
bonus credits similarly do not exist or operate in isolation. Instead, they are an integral part of, and
work together with, other aspects of the ITCs and PTCs, which provide tax credits to eligible
renewable energy facilities under certain conditions. In our view, the relationship between the Steel
and Iron Requirement – and the bonus credits more generally – and the objective of protecting
public morals can be objectively assessed only when the domestic content bonus credits are
understood in their regulatory context, as one aspect or element of a broader tax credit programme.

Just as the bonus credits do not operate in isolation, so too do we consider that their relationship
with the objective of protecting public morals must be assessed taking into account their place
within, and their operation vis-à-vis, other related aspects of the broader regulatory framework. In
so doing, we are not assessing the WTO consistency of the ITCs and PTCs generally; our inquiry is
of course limited to whether the domestic content bonus credits, which are the measures challenged
by China, fall within subparagraph (a) of Article XX.
7.166. The use of 100% domestic structural steel and iron is a condition only for receipt of the
bonus credits under the ITCs and PTCs. Thus, as China has pointed out284, the base amount of the
credit remains available even if the eligible facilities use a significant amount of Chinese steel and
iron. In our view, this means that, even if the United States' allegations concerning Chinese policies
and practices are correct, the Steel and Iron Requirement does not disincentivize, let alone restrict,
limit, or otherwise stigmatize the use of Chinese steel and iron. Neither does it send any kind of
signal to consumers that the use of Chinese steel or iron is morally problematic or contrary to the
United States' public morals against unfair competition, forced labour, theft, or coercion. To the
contrary, the ITCs and PTCs continue to reward eligible facilities even if they do use Chinese steel,
albeit at a lower rate than if they used 100% domestic steel and iron.
7.167. Thus, while the Steel and Iron Requirement evidently reflects a policy preference for the use
of domestic steel and iron, and might incentivize consumers to purchase domestic steel and iron for
economic reasons (i.e. the possibility of receiving tax credits), we do not consider that it reflects, or
could be understood by consumers as reflecting, a preference that is based on or rooted in moral
standards that are threatened or undermined by Chinese steel and iron. The United States argues
the challenged measures "signal to businesses in the U.S. marketplace that China's policies will not
be tolerated in the U.S. marketplace, thereby reducing the incentive for actors in the U.S.
marketplace to adopt or encourage behavior similar to that of China, and reducing the reward that
Chinese entities may gain from engaging in that behavior".285 In our view, whether a measure is
capable of sending such a "signal" to consumers is indeed a potentially relevant consideration to be
taken into account under Article XX(a); among other things, this consideration may be relevant to
the assessment of whether a measure aimed at limiting the consumption of goods that are not
inherently morally offensive286 is a measure capable of protecting public morals. However, for the
reasons above, we consider that the Steel and Iron Requirement, operating within and as part of its
larger regulatory framework, does not, and cannot, express any kind of moral disapprobation
regarding China's alleged unfair and non-market practices or the moral hazard that Chinese steel
and iron are alleged to represent.
7.168. As we see it, then, while the Steel and Iron Requirement, as one element of the bonus
credits, may operate as an economic inducement to use domestic products, it is incapable of
protecting public morals because it does not indicate or otherwise reveal to consumers that the
consumption of Chinese steel and iron is contrary to the United States' public morals; and, indeed,
is an element of, and works together with, a larger regulatory programme that legitimizes the
consumption of Chinese steel and iron. As a result, it appears to us that any shift in consumption
patterns towards domestic steel or iron could only be motivated by concerns unrelated to public
morals. Insofar as the bonus credits, as an element of ITCs and PTCs, are capable of motivating
consumer behaviour on the basis only of considerations that are not moral in nature, we do not

The question therefore becomes whether this aspect of the challenged measures demonstrates that

requirement could be said to contribute to the protection of the public morals invoked by the
United States. As we have observed, the Steel and Iron Requirement conditions the bonus credit
upon the use of 100% domestic steel and iron. This of course means that the bonus credit is lost
not only if an eligible facility uses structural steel or iron from China, but also if it uses such steel or
iron from any other country, including countries that the United States, in its written submissions in
this dispute, describes as "like-minded" on public morals issues, and with which it has free trade
agreements (FTAs).291

FTA partners share its commitments against unfair competition, coercion, theft, and forced labour.293
Given this, it is difficult to understand how excluding steel from those countries could contribute in

theft, and coercion, and insofar as steel and iron produced in those countries is therefore not claimed

risks rewarding China's alleged non-market practices and policies.
7.173. In our view, this premise has not been substantiated. In support of its arguments about

7.5.3.4.2 The Manufactured Products Requirement
7.184. We now turn to the second requirement that must be met in order to receive the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits, which is the Manufactured Products Requirement.
7.185. We begin by noting that the United States has not advanced arguments explaining how the
Manufactured Products Requirement specifically relates to the objective of protecting public morals.
In its written submissions, the United States refers to the ITCs and PTCs bonus credits together as
the "renewable tax credits", and argues that they "reduce reliance on China, and enhance fair
competition and market-oriented outcomes in the US market. Further, the measures incentivize
sourcing outside of China".311 In its second written submission, the United States elaborated on the
ways in which, in its view, the challenged measures are designed to protect public morals:
[T]he domestic content bonus provisions of the renewable energy tax credits also
protect public morals. As the United States has explained, the effects of China's non-
market policies and practices have been particularly profound in the steel sector, and

of the Manufactured Products Requirement as well.
7.187. In this connection, we note that, in response to a question from the Panel, the United States
argued that "the effects of China's non-market policies and practices in the steel sector also affect
iron (a key input for steel) and US manufacturing (given follow-on effects in US communities from
the closure of steelmaking facilities)". However, the United States did not elaborate or attempt to
substantiate this point.313 At any rate, and more fundamentally, the Manufactured Products
Requirement is not limited to products made from or containing steel. We therefore do not see the
connection between this requirement and the United States' concerns about Chinese alleged policies
and practices in respect of the steel sector.
7.188. In response to another question from the Panel, the United States suggested that the
Manufactured Products Requirement is concerned primarily with goods in the renewable energy
sector314, which, according to the United States, has been targeted for dominance by China.315
However, we note that the Manufactured Products Requirement is not limited to goods in that sector.
By its terms, it applies to any item produced as a result of the manufacturing process that is
incorporated into any article, material, or supply that is itself directly incorporated into an eligible

"[t]he manufactured products requirement … applies to the upstream products that become part of
a renewable energy project", and stated that "[m]anufactured products within the meaning of the

7.189. We accept that many renewable energy-related products might fall within the scope of the
Manufactured Products Requirement. It should be noted, however, that the requirement is much
broader, and is not limited in its application, either implicitly or explicitly, to goods in the sectors in
which China is alleged to behave in ways that are inconsistent with the United States' public morals.
This broad scope bears no discernible relation to the objective of protecting those public morals that
the United States alleges are threatened by goods in those specific sectors.
7.190. Even leaving this aside, we are not convinced that the Manufactured Products Requirement
is capable of protecting the United States' public morals by counteracting the effects of China's non-

must be manufactured in the United States. The percentage threshold is 40% for projects that begin
construction before 2025 and increases by 5 percentage points per year up to 55% for projects
starting construction in 2027 and beyond.320 This means that, at its most restrictive, (from 2027

7.191. In the first place, and like the Steel and Iron Requirement, the Manufactured Products
Requirement is an element of, and works together with, the other aspects of the ITCs and PTCs.
While the Manufactured Products Requirement applies to the bonus credit, the base credit remains
available to facilities that incorporate very few or even no products manufactured in the
United States (and thus, presumably, many or even only manufactured products produced in China).
For the reasons we explained in relation to the Steel and Iron Requirement, we do not think that a
measure can be said to be designed or taken to protect public morals when it does not stigmatize,
and even rewards, the use of the very products that are alleged to be inconsistent with the public
morals at issue. The Manufactured Products Requirement, operating within and as part of its larger
regulatory framework, does not express any kind of moral disapprobation regarding China's alleged
unfair and non-market practices in the renewable energy or any other sector.
7.192. On the contrary, while it provides an economic incentive to use domestic products, it
continues to mark Chinese products as legitimate market options. If, therefore, it creates an
incentive to use domestic goods, it does not, and cannot, do so for any explicit or implicit moral
reasons, and changes in consumption patterns could therefore only be motived by reasons unrelated
to the establishment or maintenance of standards of right and wrong conduct, as consumers would
be entirely incapable of apprehending that the use of such Chinese manufactured products is
inconsistent with the United States' standards of right and wrong conduct. In such circumstances,
we do not see how the Manufactured Products Requirement can be said to be capable of protecting
public morals.
7.193. Moreover, we note that the Manufactured Products Requirement does not require the use of
100% domestic manufactured products. Instead, it requires a certain percentage of domestic
content that increases from 40% for projects that begin construction before 2025 up to 55% for
projects starting construction in 2027 and beyond. This means that, even at its more restrictive, the
Manufactured Products Requirement can be satisfied where up to 45% of manufactured components
are manufactured in countries other than the United States, including China. It is therefore not only
the regulatory programme of which the Manufactured Products Requirement forms part but the
requirement itself that continues to legitimize and reward the consumption of the very goods that
the United States submits represent a moral hazard. In the light of this circumstance, we cannot
accept the United States' argument that the "bonus credits protect US public morals because they
ensure that US support – through tax credits – does not reward China's non-market policies and
practices".321 As we have explained, the ITCs and PTCs generally and the Manufactured Products
Requirement specifically seem to us to do precisely that.

not merely require that, in order to protect the United States' public morals against China's alleged

content must be used. In response to a question from the Panel on this issue, the United States
explained that "[r]equiring manufacturing in the United States plays a significant role in protecting
US public morals because manufacturers in the United States must comply with the US constitution,
the Sherman Act, the Federal Trade Commission Act, US labour laws, and other laws that reflect US
public morals".322 No doubt this is true. But it fails to explain why manufacturers in countries other
than China, and at least those "like-minded" countries that share the United States' commitments
against unfair competition, coercion, theft, and forced labour, would not be subject to equivalent
laws. Certainly, the United States has not provided any evidence that this is not the case.
7.195. We note, moreover, that the United States has consistently defended the challenged
measures, including the Manufactured Products Requirement, on the basis that they are designed
and necessary to "counteract[] the effects of China's non-market policies and practices".323 It has
not argued that manufactured products, including in the renewable energy sector, that are

requiring a higher proportion of domestic inputs, when a substantial portion of foreign inputs is

begins before 2025, then it is not clear why a higher percentage is required to achieve the same

7.197. We are mindful that, in response to a question from the Panel concerning the Manufactured

[T]he fact that some content may originate outside the United States, including from
China, does not render the measures at issue inconsistent with US public morals

are structured so as to avoid US purchasers rewarding Chinas non-market policies and
practices that violate U.S. norms against unfair competition, forced labor, theft, and

7.198. However, for the reasons we have explained, we do not consider that the domestic content
requirements in the ITCs and PTCs are "are structured so as to avoid US purchasers rewarding
China's non-market policies and practices". On the contrary, the challenged measures continue to
reward consumers for using Chinese products, and are therefore incapable of incentivizing behaviour
on the basis of standards of right and wrong conduct. Quite simply, by allowing "some content" to
originate in China, the Manufactured Products Requirement is incapable of providing any indication
to consumers that the use of Chinese products entails or involves moral considerations or has
implications for the realization or protection of standards of right and wrong conduct.
7.199. The United States concluded its response to a further question about the ITCs and PTCs by
arguing that "the measures incentivize US production and reduce dependence on and exposure to
Chinese steel, iron, and manufactured products, thereby protecting US public morals against unfair
competition, forced labour, theft, and coercion".326 In our view, the mere fact that a certain measure
incentivizes or increases domestic production or reduces dependence on and exposure to a certain
trading partner does not, on its own or without more, show that the measure was designed or taken
to protect public morals. Those outcomes may be the goal or result of various kinds of objectives,
perhaps including moral objectives, but certainly also including economic objectives that have no
moral implications.327 Thus, the fact that a certain measure may, for example, increase domestic
production does not, in itself, establish that the measure was designed to protect public morals. The
question is not whether the measure produces a certain economic outcome, but whether the design

7.200. In this dispute, therefore, the relevant inquiry is not whether the challenged measures are
designed to increase domestic production or reduce reliance on China, but whether they are designed
to protect the United States' public morals against unfair competition, forced labour, theft, and
coercion. The former may be a relevant factor in assessing the latter, but the two inquiries are not
coterminous. The fact that a measure has a certain economic outcome is not a pertinent answer to
doubts about whether that measure was taken to protect public morals that arise from an objective
assessment of its design and structure. We have identified several aspects of the design and
structure of the Manufactured Products Requirement that cast doubt on whether it is designed to
protect public morals. And we are not convinced that the United States has explained these aspects
in a way that is sufficient to remove those doubts.
7.201. For these reasons, several aspects of the design, structure, and expected operation of the
Manufactured Products Requirement contradict the United States' assertion that the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits are measures taken to protect public morals.
7.5.3.5 Conclusion on whether the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are
measures "to protect public morals"
7.202. The United States has the burden of establishing that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus
Credits are measures taken "to protect public morals" within the meaning of Article XX(a) of the
GATT 1994.
7.203. For the reasons above, we consider that there is evidence before us that contradicts the
United States' assertion that "targeting" certain sectors for dominance and engaging in state-
directed investment could reasonably be regarded as falling within the scope of the United States'
purported public morals against either "unfair competition" or "coercion", even assuming such public
morals exists in the abstract in US society. We further consider that the direct evidence pertaining
to the objectives of the measures at issue submitted by the United States contradicts its assertion
that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are measures taken to protect public morals.
Additionally, several aspects of the design, structure, and expected operation of the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits further contradict the United States' assertion that the ITC/PTC
Domestic Content Bonus Credits are measures taken to protect public morals. Based on these
considerations taken together, none of which we regard as necessarily decisive, our global
assessment is that the United States has not demonstrated that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content
Bonus Credits are measures necessary for the protection of public morals under the general
exception in Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994.
7.5.4 Remaining issues in respect of the United States' Article XX(a) defence
7.204. We have concluded that the United States has not demonstrated that the measures at issue
are taken "to protect public morals" based on the nature of the alleged public morals invoked by the
United States, the direct evidence of the objectives of the measures at issue, and the existence of a
relationship between the alleged public morals and the design, structure, and expected operation of
the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits.
7.205. Accordingly, and recalling that the WTO dispute settlement system is not meant "to
encourage either panels or the Appellate Body to 'make law' by clarifying existing provisions of the
WTO Agreement outside the context of resolving a particular dispute"328, it is unnecessary for us to
make any definitive findings on the types of values or interests that may qualify as "public morals"
under Article XX(a), including the relevant interpretative issues raised by the arguments of the
parties and third parties in this dispute.329

7.206. Likewise, because the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits fail to meet this threshold
legal element, we do not need to assess the remaining legal elements of the "necessity" test under
Article XX(a) to determine the applicability of this exception.
7.207. Since the challenged measures have not been shown to fall under Article XX(a) and are thus
not provisionally justified under this subparagraph, it is also not necessary for us to assess whether
the measures have been applied consistently with the requirements of the chapeau of Article XX.330
7.208. Finally, in the light of our finding that the measures are not justified under Article XX(a) of
the GATT 1994, we do not need to rule on whether the general exceptions in Article XX are applicable
to the SCM Agreement. This is so because the United States' invocation of Article XX(a) vis-à-vis all
the relevant covered agreements rests on the same evidence and arguments. The United States has
not advanced, and we do not discern, any way in which the United States' invocation of Article XX(a)
of the GATT 1994 could fail vis-à-vis the GATT 1994 but succeed in the context of the SCM
Agreement (or the TRIMs Agreement).331
7.5.5 Conclusion
7.209. For the reasons stated above, we conclude that the United States has not demonstrated that
the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are measures necessary for the protection of public
morals under the general exception in Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994.
8 CONCLUSIONS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
8.1. For the reasons set forth in this Report, the Panel concludes that:
a. the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with Article III:4 of the
GATT 1994 because by making eligibility for bonus subsidy amounts conditional on the
use of US-origin goods, the United States does not accord to products of Chinese origin
treatment no less favourable than the treatment accorded to like products of national
origin in respect of laws, regulations, and requirements affecting the internal sale, offering
for sale, purchase, transportation, distribution or use of the affected products;
b. the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with Article 2.1 of the TRIMs
Agreement, because the conditions are investment measures related to trade in goods
that are inconsistent with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994;
c. the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2
of the SCM Agreement because they are subsidies granted or maintained by the
United States contingent, whether solely or as one of several other conditions, upon the
use of domestic over imported goods; and
d. the United States has not demonstrated that the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits
are measures necessary for the protection of public morals under the general exception in
Article XX(a) of the GATT 1994.
8.2. Under Article 3.8 of the DSU, in cases where there is an infringement of the obligations
assumed under a covered agreement, the action is considered prima facie to constitute a case of
nullification or impairment. The Panel concludes that, to the extent that the ITC/PTC Domestic
Content Bonus Credits are inconsistent with the GATT 1994, the TRIMs Agreement, and the
SCM Agreement, they have nullified or impaired benefits accruing to China under those agreements.

8.3. Pursuant to Article 19.1 of the DSU, the Panel recommends that the United States bring the
ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits into conformity with Article III:4 of the GATT 1994 and
Article 2.1 of the TRIMs Agreement.
8.4. Pursuant to Article 4.7 of the SCM Agreement, and in the light of its findings under
Articles 3.1(b) and 3.2 of the SCM Agreement, the Panel further recommends that the United States
withdraw the ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits without delay.
8.5. Article 4.7 of the SCM Agreement directs us to specify, in our recommendation, the time-period
within which these measures must be withdrawn. We recognize that the United States'
implementation of our recommendation would require legislative action to modify the relevant
provisions of US tax law, and we also understand that the US fiscal year 2027 starts on 1 October
2026, although the parties disagree regarding the significance of the fiscal year.332 In the
circumstances of this case, that date appears a reasonable deadline for the withdrawal of the
ITC/PTC Domestic Content Bonus Credits. Given the timing of the issuance of this Report, we
consider that this deadline will provide sufficient time to take relevant legislative action, and it also
accommodates any fiscal-year issues identified by the United States with respect to these tax
measures. We note that the United States has provided no reasoning as to why legislative action
could not be accomplished in such a time frame. While we do not consider that it would always be
appropriate to set a time-period that would allow a responding Member to continue to grant
prohibited subsidies through to the end of the fiscal year in which circulation and adoption of a panel
report takes place, we note that granting the United States until 30 September 2026 also finds
support in the approach taken by the panel in US – FSC.333
8.6. Accordingly, the Panel recommends that the United States withdraw the ITC/PTC Domestic
Content Bonus Credits by 1 October 2026 at the latest.
